title: Capacity consolidation policy and prioritization for dedicated enterprise requests
preview:
Problem: Enterprise customers submit dedicated capacity requests that are highly variable — many small, overlapping requests and some burst-only asks — leading to fragmented reservations, inefficient GPU allocations, and missed SLA targets. This ticket defines a consolidation policy and prioritization guidance to reduce fragmentation, improve utilization, and align reserved throughput and GPU class decisions with clear enterprise SLAs and scaling expectations.

Scope: intake time prioritization rubric, request bundling rules, GPU fitment recommendations (A/B/C bands), temporal smoothing (monthly windowing and predictable burst windows), and fallbacks when capacity or GPU class is constrained. This is product-facing guidance for intake gating and decisioning; implementation will require downstream ENG follow-up for automation and console UI changes.

title: Cycle Pulse & Echo: Capacity Canvas and Slate Shepherding
preview: Problem: In high-velocity cycles we repeatedly hit two friction points: (1) teams over-commit early and then scramble to de-scope mid-cycle, and (2) platform ops can't reliably forecast burst capacity needs for Dedicated on short notice. Scope: establish a repeatable lightweight process and artifacts (Capacity Canvas + Slate Shepherd) that combine quantitative capacity budgeting with qualitative tradeoff surfaces so PMs and Eng leads can make deterministic cycle choices. Goals: reduce mid-cycle scope churn by 30%, shorten decision turnaround for staffing reallocations to <48h, and provide a 2-week predictive window for Dedicated capacity requests. Approach: introduce "Pulse" checkpoints (pre-cycle, week2 pulse, mid-cycle pulse) with clear signals and an "Echo" reserve policy (micro-credits pool + shadow roster). Deliverables: (1) Capacity Canvas template (workload-weighted token-hours, critical-path roles, cross-team friction points), (2) Slate Shepherding rubric (decomposition rules, soft/hard deltas, canary gating), (3) Runbook for triggering micro-credit allocations and emergency de-scope flows, (4) Dashboard cards and alert thresholds in Console to show capacity burn and reserve projections. Tradeoffs: simpler heuristics reduce cognitive load but may underfit highly variable infra bursts; we include a small modelling component to flag low-confidence forecasts. Stakeholders: PMs, Eng leads, SRE, People Ops for short-term staffing, Solutions for Dedicated customer communications. Success metrics: mid-cycle scope changes reduced; average time-to-allocate staff from pool; Dedicated capacity requests predicted 2 weeks ahead with >75% accuracy. This ticket covers articulation of templates, decision criteria, small pilot with two initiatives, and tooling hooks for alerts. Links to artifacts and PRs included below.

title: Developer Onboarding Funnel & SDK Playbook — sample apps, guided flows, and measurement
preview:
Summary:
This initiative creates a repeatable onboarding funnel and operational playbook to improve first-time SDK activation, reduce time-to-first-success, and establish measurement for SDK + docs + sample apps. Outcome: a documented playbook, 3 production-ready sample apps (Python, JS, Go), a guided 'first-call' interactive quickstart in Console, and an analytics schema to track funnel conversion and retention.

Goals / OKRs: 
- OKR 1: Increase SDK activation rate (trial accounts completing first successful API call with SDK) from 22% -> 45% in Q2.
- OKR 2: Reduce median time-to-first-success from 22 minutes -> <10 minutes.
- OKR 3: Create an evaluation signal for SDK regressions: deploy a weekly smoke test suite covering top 10 endpoints.

Scope & Deliverables (top-level):
1) SDK onboarding playbook (PM + Eng + Design): step-by-step flows for sign-up -> token -> local quickstart -> sample app deployment. Includes decision trees for private/dedicated customers.
2) Three opinionated sample apps: minimal web demo (JS), batch embed pipeline (Python), lightweight inference microservice (Go). Each with CI, Dockerfiles, and GitHub Actions publishing to packages registry.
3) Console-guided first-call flow: embedded terminal/Code Runner + copy-into-editor flow that wires API keys securely with ephemeral tokens.
4) Metrics & instrumentation: analytics schema (events + properties), dashboards for activation funnel (signup -> key creation -> sdk install -> first-call success), and retention cohorts.
5) Playbook docs: Confluence playbook + publishable docs site pages and a 'Runbook for SDK regression' for on-call.

Key decisions & trade-offs: 
- Prioritize Python and JS sample apps for GA; Go sample app scoped as alpha (fewer clicks) due to engineering bandwidth.
- Console-guided flow will use ephemeral token service (short TTL) to avoid long-lived secrets in user browsers. This requires a small infra change (ENG-3928).
- We will not attempt full interactive REPL for private/on-prem in this cycle; provide an equivalent CLI-template instead and mark as TODO in the playbook.

Progress updates (high level):
- 2026-02-20: Kickoff completed; cross-functional roster confirmed (PM, Eng SDK, Infra, Design, Docs, Analytics).
- 2026-02-28: Design delivered three initial UX mockups for the Console 'first-call' flow; reviewed with security for token design (DES-1284). Feedback: add explicit consent copy and a rotate-key CTA.
- 2026-03-04: Engineering spike completed on ephemeral token service: prototype shows acceptable latency; dependency created (ENG-3928).
- 2026-03-08: Docs team pushed initial drafts for sample app READMEs and playbook outline to Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.example/display/DEVX/SDK+Onboarding+Playbook (internal).
- 2026-03-10: Began assembling analytics schema; draft event list and property definitions committed: sdk_install, sdk_import_run, first_call_success, sample_app_deploy, console_first_call_started.

Open blockers / risks: 
- Infra capacity for ephemeral token service during peak onboarding demos (risk: Console first-call latency). Mitigation: ephemeral tokens will be cached per-region for 30s and autoscaled; infra owner ENG-3928.
- Sample app packaging: single-source-of-truth for templates required to avoid divergent examples; solution: create a template repo and a thin generator (PM to review).

Success criteria & measurement: 
- Instrumentation in GA: funnels visible in LookerStudio and live dashboard within 2 weeks of sample app GA.
- Activation conversion > 40% within 6 weeks post release for newly created trial orgs.
- Time-to-first-success median < 10 minutes from sign-up for interactive guided flow.

Artifacts & links: 
- Confluence playbook (draft): https://confluence.redwood.example/display/DEVX/SDK+Onboarding+Playbook
- PR: SDK python quickstart: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-python/pull/421 (internal)
- PR: Console first-call UI: https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/998 (internal)
- Analytics schema draft: https://docs.redwood.example/analytics/sdk-onboarding-schema (internal)

Next milestones / timeline: 
- 2026-03-18: Complete Python sample app GA candidate + CI publishing.
- 2026-03-25: Console guided-flow beta deployed to staging + smoke-tests running.
- 2026-03-31: Publish playbook and docs site pages + begin limited beta with 50 new trial orgs.
- 2026-04-15: Evaluate metrics and iterate for broader rollout.

title: Dynamic batch-slot experiment and QSA gap analysis for per-vector pricing
preview: Goal: validate a dynamic batch-slot pricing model that reduces per-vector unit cost for high-throughput customers while preserving latency SLOs and rerank quality. title: Enterprise SDK adapter matrix: streaming ergonomics & auth convergence
preview:
Summary:
Consolidate incoming enterprise requests for language-specific SDK adapters and streaming authentication ergonomics into a prioritized, pragmatic roadmap and a repeatable triage/playbook. Customers are asking for better first‑class experience across: Java/.NET/Go/Rust bindings, resumable streaming with token rotation, client-side reconnect semantics, and enterprise auth patterns (SAML/OIDC token exchange, mTLS, client cert rotation).

Goals:
- Produce a prioritization rubric that balances enterprise security needs, integration complexity, and business impact.
- Define an adapter matrix (language x feature surface) that yields a minimal viable set of SDKs + streaming primitives for high-risk customers.
- Provide engineering-friendly acceptance criteria (API contract, test harness, telemetry) so engineering can scope and plan.
- Surface security and rollout constraints early (KMS integration, audit logs, SSO flows).

Scope & constraints:
- Non-bug feature triage only (new SDK adapters, streaming auth ergonomics, client-side reconnection/rotation).
- Focus on enterprise onramp use-cases (high-privacy customers, strict audit/KMS requirements).
- Avoid full language parity initially; emphasize adapters that unblock largest customers and platform teams.

Proposed prioritization heuristic (triage rubric highlights):
1) Security sensitivity (does customer require confidential computing, KMS/HSM integration, or strict token rotation?) – high weight
2) Expected concurrent streaming sessions and latency SLOs – medium weight
3) Customer ARR / strategic value & referenceability – medium weight
4) Implementation complexity (bindings, runtime differences, native deps) – negative weight
5) Maintenance cost (surface area, backward-compatibility, CI footprint) – negative weight

Initial recommended priorities (MVP set):
- P0 (Q2): Java SDK adapter + streaming resumable token protocol (enterprise TLS + token rotation flows) — high enterprise demand, many platform teams run on JVM stacks
- P1 (Q3): .NET SDK with explicit SSO/OIDC exchange examples and token refresh helpers
- P2 (Q3): Go SDK with lightweight streaming client and connection-retry primitives (low-latency services)
- P3 (Future): Rust binding (customer-driven) and language community contributions via adapter template

Risk & mitigations:
- Security gating may delay initial rollout -> Mitigate by designing an isolated feature flag/fallback and conducting an early security review.
- Eng cost for native dependencies -> Provide a C-core + thin language wrappers to reduce duplication.
- Breaking changes to streaming protocol -> Maintain stable handshake v1 and design v2 with opt-in semantics.

Deliverables (MVP):
- Adapter matrix doc mapping: language -> supported features (streaming resume, token rotation, mTLS, example integration)
- Triage rubric spreadsheet used to score pending requests
- API contract doc for streaming handshake & resumable sessions (example gRPC/websocket and REST fallbacks)
- Minimal Java SDK prototype with streaming resume and token refresh helpers + integration test harness
- Security checklist and audit-playbook for enterprise enablement

Success metrics (first 90 days post-launch):
- Two enterprise customers onboarded using the Java adapter with streaming resume enabled
- 50% reduction in engineering discovery time for enterprise onboarding requests (measured via intake cycle time)
- Telemetry: token rotation errors < 0.5% of streams; reconnect success rate > 98%

title: Capacity reservoir + tenant isolation controls for Dedicated clusters (PRD)
preview: This PRD defines the capacity reservoir concept and tenant isolation controls for Redwood Dedicated clusters. The goal is to provide predictable reserved throughput for paying customers while enabling bounded overflow (bursts) and strong isolation guarantees to prevent noisy-neighbor and overload scenarios. The design balances utilization efficiency with SLA fidelity and operational simplicity.

The goal is to provide predictable reserved throughput for paying customers while enabling bounded overflow (bursts) and strong isolation guarantees to prevent noisy-neighbor and overload scenarios. The design balances utilization efficiency with SLA fidelity and operational simplicity.

title: Model Terrain Pulse — procurement trigger experiments & cohort signal playbook
preview:
Goal: produce an operational playbook and prototype dashboards that translate continuous market signals (model capability, latency & pricing shifts, regional availability) into deterministic procurement triggers and prioritized follow-up work.

Why now: sales + customer success reported three recent prospect losses due to sudden provider availability and pricing swings. We need a repeatable, low-friction system to detect when a supplier/model requires procurement action (e.g., reserved capacity, switch, or contract negotiation).

Scope / Deliverables:
- Signal taxonomy & scoring rubric: define 8 signals (availability, price delta, latency delta, quantization support, license change, region outage, API contract mismatch, empirical quality regression) and an algorithmic score that maps to actions (monitor, engage partner, escalate to sales).
- Cohort mapping: categorize customers/workloads into 4 procurement cohorts (latency-sensitive, cost-sensitive, regulated, edge/RAG).
- Prototype dashboard spec: time-series & event overlay, alert thresholds, suggested remediation steps, and a sample procurement-execution checklist. Link to spec: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/ModelTerrainPulseSpec
- Synthetic workload tests: generate three workload profiles and run model substitution experiments to estimate expected customer impact and unit-cost delta. Results to be published as CSV and a short report.
- Operational runbook: PR trigger flows, Slack alert format, escalation owners, and handoff to sales/solutions.

Acceptance criteria:
- Scoring rubric documented and reviewed by PM, Eng, and Sales (sign-off in comments).
- Prototype dashboard mocked and validated with Design.
- Signal ingestion pipeline MVP ingesting at least two telemetry sources (model catalog updates + cloud price feeds).
- Three synthetic workload substitution experiments completed with baseline delta table.

Success metrics (first 90 days):
- Time-to-detect for supply shocks less than 6 hours (from feed -> alert) for signals with high confidence.
- At least one procurement action that avoids a customer-facing degradation (tracked in CRM).
- False positive alert rate < 15% for production customers in the pilot cohort.

Plan & milestones:
- Week 1 (2026-02-25 to 2026-03-03): finalize signals and cohorts; align with Sales and Infra (completed).
- Week 2: dashboard spec + design mockups; telemetry mapping (in progress).
- Week 3: MVP ingestion + scoring engine prototype; run 3 synthetic experiments (blocked on ENG-22344 GPU quota request).
- Week 4: pilot run with 5 customers in the regulated cohort; collect feedback and runbook iteration.

Risks & trade-offs:
- Signal noise: model capability changes can be noisy; to reduce churn we will require multi-source confirmation before triggering high-severity actions.
- Telemetry gaps: some providers don't publish timely price/region feeds — fallback to empirical probes (synthetic workloads) increases cost.
- Procurement latency: operational buy-in is required; the playbook must be simple and prescriptive to be effective.

Data sources and artifacts:
- Model catalog changefeed (internal Kafka topic: models.catalog.changes)
- Public cloud price feeds (GCP/AWS/Azure snapshots)
- Synthetic workload harness: https://github.com/redwood-inference/model-terrain-harness (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/model-terrain-harness/pull/42)
- Dashboard mock: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/ModelTerrainPulseMocks

Stakeholders:
- Product: Ava Chen (owner), Liam O'Rourke (PM lead)
- Eng: Priya Kapoor (runtime), Marcos Silva (telemetry)
- Design: Zoe Martinez (dashboards)
- Sales/Ops: Nora Patel (enterprise sales), Diego Ramos (solutions)

Next steps for assignee:
1) Finalize telemetry schema with Marcos by 2026-03-12.
2) Book design sync with Zoe and provide sample data.
3) Coordinate GPU quota request resolution for synthetic tests with Infra (ENG-22344).

Notes: this ticket focuses on productizing the detection + recommendation layer; actual procurement execution is out of scope and will be handed to Sales/Ops via the runbook.

title: Field Alpha Probe Program: model liveness & sourcing cost tracker
preview:
Objective: run a lightweight, repeatable "alpha probe" program to validate model liveness, true-to-production sourcing cost, and procurement friction signals for 6 target open and partner models across regulated verticals (finserv, healthcare, legal).

Why: existing market scans surface candidate models but do not capture operational liveness (how often binaries/weights are pulled/updated or deprecated), procurement latency (time to license/confirm usage terms), nor real per-customer cost to serve under production-like token distributions. These signals materially affect fallback strategy and Dedicated capacity commitments.

Scope and deliverables: compact playbook and dataset: 1) A runbook for a 6-week alpha probe executed by PM + Solutions Eng + Procurement; 2) A reproducible telemetry harness that measures: liveness (availability checks, patch cadence), provisioning latency (time-to-get-keys/contracts), cost-to-serve estimates (token-based simulation using our latency/cost profiles), licensing risk notes, and model-quality quick-checks on a 200-case prompt corpus per vertical; 3) A scoring rubric (LivenessScore, ProcurementLagDays, ServeCostUSD_per_month_estimate, QualityDelta) to feed the model-supply dashboard; 4) A short go/no-go recommendation template for whether to onboard or keep as fallback.

Method: use a mix of synthetic traffic (to emulate 95/99p seq lengths), partner pilot calls (where allowed), and public artifact monitoring (GitHub, model hub changelogs). Coordinate procurement to attempt minimal license agreements in parallel to capture real legal/ops timings.

Key success metrics: automated liveness checks run weekly with >= 5 data points per model; procurement latency measured end-to-end for at least 4 models; cost simulation validated against one Dedicated pilot; rubric yields actionable binary go/no-go for 75% of probed models.

Acceptance criteria: (1) Playbook published to Confluence with run steps + ownership; (2) Telemetry repo with scripts and synthetic workload definition merged to GitHub; (3) Dashboard tile in supply-monitor shows the four rubric signals for all 6 models; (4) One pilot procurement completes during the alpha window and documents negotiation blockers.

Notes on constraints and trade-offs: we will not attempt full-scale quality evaluations (LLM fine-grained metrics) in this alpha — that is a separate eval effort. We must balance synthetic workload realism vs. cost: prefer sampled real customer prompts when partner pilots are not available. Procurement experiments may be blocked in some regions — log as data point rather than blocker.

title: Release-train comms, ops runbook, and release-notes digest for API/Console/Runtime
preview:
Summary: Coordinate a single, consolidated release-train communications and operational runbook that covers milestones, scope-lock signals, cut criteria, release-notes generation, and GTM tasks across API, Console, and Runtime teams. Goal is to reduce ambiguity about last-minute scope decisions, automate a digest for release notes, and codify a lightweight hypercare plan for the first 72 hours post-rollout.

Background: Recent rollouts had fragmented comms between runtime and console teams resulting in mismatched docs, late bugfix rollouts, and duplicated rollback efforts. This initiative centralizes cutline decisions and creates an automated release-notes digest that compiles: PRs merged after freeze, critical known issues, upgrade notes, and per-team owner blurbs.

Scope:
- Milestone calendar and signoff checklist for MAJOR, MINOR, and PATCH release lanes.
- Cut criteria matrix (functional, perf, security, docs) and who has veto authority.
- Release-notes digest template and automation spec (GitHub action + Confluence integration).
- Communication matrix with channel templates (Slack, email, release board) and stakeholders.
- 72-hour hypercare plan including on-call rotation, SLA escalations, and rollback playbook.
- Postmortem and evaluation plan for quality/regression metrics tied to the release.

Out of scope: Full localization of release notes (will be addressed as a follow-up), long-form marketing collateral, and marketplace listings updates.

title: Acceptance criteria for structured response protocols and fallback modes
preview: Purpose: Define deterministic, testable acceptance criteria for structured output (JSON/typed responses) and tool/function-calling behavior across three operational modes: synchronous full-response, streaming partial-response, and graceful fallback/error envelopes. This ticket captures requirements clarifications, scope decisions, and explicit testable outcomes the product and engineering teams must satisfy before we mark Structured Output v2 as shippable. The doc focuses on schema negotiation, validation tolerances, error/fallback semantics, streaming checkpoints, and telemetry needed for rollouts.

title: LandingPad sprint allocator and release-train governance
preview:
Problem statement: As multiple feature teams target overlapping launch windows across runtime, console, and API surfaces, we lack a single, enforceable runway allocator that ties launch dates to capacity reservations, OKR deadlines, and rollback-safe guardrails. This creates last-minute contention for Dedicated capacity, elevated latency risk, and misaligned communications for enterprise customers.

Goal: Build the "LandingPad" sprint allocator — a lightweight governance pattern and supporting coordination checklist that: (1) maps launches to capacity lanes (baseline, hedge, emergency), (2) enforces gating criteria per surface (runtime/console/api), (3) publishes a one-page cross-team runway with rollout owners and rollback triggers, and (4) integrates with quarterly OKRs and the release calendar so product planning and platform capacity are aligned.

Scope and deliverables:
- Define LandingPad governance doc (roles, lanes, gating matrix).
- Create a templated runway artifact in Confluence that PMs can claim per feature and which auto-summarizes capacity needs.
- Lightweight signals & thresholds for autoscaling/capacity reservations (input to ENG tickets ENG-9841/ENG-12012).
- A Slack release-train channel template and weekly cadence (owner rotation) for cross-surface go/no-go.
- Run a pilot with two teams (runtime model-quantization rollout and console reporting migration) during the 2026.04 train.

Non-goals: We will not build a full scheduling automation engine in this ticket — this is governance + templates + two pilots. Full automation will be a follow-up if pilots show >30% reduction in contention events.

title: Procurement Trust Sandbox: SOC2-Minimalist Package + Insurance Offset Pricing Pilot
preview: Run a 6-week pilot that packages a lightweight, machine-readable SOC2 evidence bundle and an insurance-offset credit product for low-to-medium risk vendors. The goal is to validate whether (a) offering a 'Trust Sandbox' — a self-serve minimal SOC2 artifact package + optional short-term insurance offset — reduces procurement lead time and increases conversion for mid-market customers, and (b) which pricing shape maximizes revenue while keeping legal and risk exposure bounded.

title: Calibration run priority queue, artifact staging, and hot-swap workflow for quant profiles
preview:
Problem: As calibration runs (FP8/INT8 sweeps) scale across tenants and model variants, we need a robust orchestration layer in the Console to prioritize runs, stage resulting quant-profiles as promoted or ephemeral artifacts, and support hot-swap promotion without service disruption. Current behavior queues runs FIFO and writes artifacts directly to long-term storage which causes delays for critical runs and complicates rollbacks and permissioning.

Goal: Implement a priority-aware queue with tenant and SLA awareness, an intermediate staging area for candidate quant profiles (ephemeral with TTL), and a hot-swap promotion path that updates serving routing without requiring tenant downtime. The feature must include RBAC controls for who can promote artifacts, audit logging for promotions, and retention/GC policies for staged artifacts.

title: Policy Canvas: region priority & tier-fallback visual configurator
preview: Create a Console-first visual editor ("Policy Canvas") that lets customers author routing policies combining region selection, customer tier routing, and fallback model variants. The editor should: 1) surface region priority lists with weighted preference and automatic compatibility checks, 2) allow tier mapping (e.g., free/prod/enterprise) to model variants and region tiers, 3) configure fallback variants (quantized/cheaper models or lower-tier regions) with stacked priority, 4) run live validation and a preview simulation that shows example request routing across a sample traffic profile, and 5) generate a machine-readable policy JSON for the runtime while preserving a human-friendly visual layout.

title: Post-pilot readout + next-steps plan — Vertical ABM (Regulated Workloads: FinServ + Healthcare)
preview:
Goal
Create a durable post-pilot readout covering (1) pipeline outcomes, (2) lessons learned / what changed in messaging + process, (3) what assets converted, and (4) a proposal to scale ABM to additional regulated segments without pulling in major product scope.

Audience
Exec staff (Marketing + Sales + Product), Security/GRC, SE leadership. This is intended to become the “v1 playbook” artifact for repeatable regulated outbound.

Pilot snapshot
- Timebox: ~8 weeks (prep + execution + close) May–June 2025
- Verticals: Financial services (primary) + Healthcare/PHI-adjacent (secondary)
- Primary products: Redwood Dedicated + Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem)
- Core buyer set: CISO/Security, Platform/ML Eng, Procurement

Success criteria (from brief)
- Validate repeatable positioning + outbound motion for compliance-driven buyers
- Produce a baseline set of vertical assets (landing page, one-pagers, security FAQ/evidence pack index, talk tracks, sequences)
- Generate qualified pipeline (meetings, SQLs, pilots/POCs, conversion to procurement)

Outcomes (high-level)
Pipeline / meetings
- Target accounts: 38 total (FinServ: 24, Healthcare: 14)
- Coverage: 32/38 had a named owner + first touch within first 10 business days of execution
- Positive replies: 21 accounts (55% of targeted accounts) had at least one positive signal (reply, referral, security questionnaire request, or “send materials”) 
- Meetings booked: 14 (FinServ: 9, Healthcare: 5)
- SQLs created: 6 (FinServ: 4, Healthcare: 2)
- POCs started: 2 (Northbridge Banking, Helix Health System)
- Procurement/legal engaged: 1 (Trident Financial Services)

Qualitative signals
- “Regulated-ready” posture consistently drove the first conversion event (security call / evidence request), not performance claims alone.
- The best-performing hook was: predictable throughput + compliance controls + deployment flexibility (VPC/on-prem) framed as “avoid building and operating inference infrastructure under audit constraints.”
- Most late-stage risk was not product gaps; it was response-time + consistency on security questionnaires and commercial packaging clarity (Dedicated vs Private vs hosted, plus retention/residency caveats).

Account outcomes (not exhaustive; focus on stage movement)
- Northbridge Banking (FinServ): POC complete; success criteria met on latency + logging requirements; next step is a rollout plan + procurement discovery (Dedicated vs Private decision leaning Private/VPC due to internal network policy).
- Helix Health System (Healthcare): POC scoped + executed; outcome was “possible” but additional PHI guardrails and clearer retention/residency controls needed for full go-live; next step is security follow-up + procurement timing alignment.
- Brightline Insurance (FinServ): security review completed; evidence pack requested; next step is a short technical deep dive (audit log export format + SAML/SCIM) before committing to POC.
- Regal Wealth Management (FinServ): technical deep dive completed on audit logs + KMS/HSM; strong fit signal; waiting on internal platform team bandwidth to scope pilot.
- Trident Financial Services (FinServ): progressed to procurement/legal; redlines in flight (DPA/SLA + residency language); outcome TBD.

What we shipped (assets + enablement)
Assets produced/updated during the pilot (v1)
- Regulated workloads landing page (with cross-links to Security FAQ + evidence pack index)
- FinServ messaging framework v1
- Healthcare messaging framework v1
- Regulated workloads security FAQ (sales-ready)
- Evidence pack index page (request/sharing guidance)
- Regulated Workloads ABM deck v1
- Regulated inference competitive battlecard
- Outbound email sequence (FinServ) + LinkedIn ads creative brief
- Webinar outline + follow-up templates (webinar not executed within pilot window; outline used for 1:1 talk track)

Asset conversion (directional; pull exact counts from dashboard spec)
- Highest-converting assets in “first conversion event” (meeting / security call):
  1) Security FAQ (link sent in outbound follow-up) 
  2) Evidence pack index (created confidence + made the request process concrete)
  3) ABM deck (used in live calls; reduced time to “next step”)
- Landing page performance was “good enough” for ABM but not yet a scalable demand gen engine; the CTA that worked best was “Book a security + architecture review” over “Download benchmark.”

Messaging learnings (what worked / what we changed)
What worked
- Lead with auditability + control: audit logs, encryption/KMS, SSO/SAML, data handling boundaries.
- Pair compliance with economics: “predictable throughput and cost controls under real traffic,” not generic “fast GPUs.”
- Emphasize deployment flexibility as a risk reducer: Hosted → Dedicated → Private/VPC/on-prem as they mature.

What did not work (or triggered friction)
- “HIPAA-ready” language in healthcare; too loaded without strict qualification and legal review. Replaced with “PHI-adjacent workflows” + “supports common security controls; details depend on deployment and customer configuration.”
- Overly model-centric positioning; buyers cared more about operational posture (logs, access controls, change management, retention).

Top objections and effective responses
- “We need on-prem / no public internet access” → Redwood Private deployment pattern + customer-managed networking; SE-led architecture review.
- “We need audit logs exported to our SIEM” → show log fields + export options; clarify timeline for any format specifics; point to examples.
- “We need strict data retention controls” → clarify current retention policy per deployment and what is customer-configurable; align on requirements early.
- “We already have GPUs” → frame Redwood as the inference runtime + control plane + operational tooling (rollouts, fallback, observability) and faster path to SLA compliance.

Process learnings (internal)
What went well
- Weekly governance with Sales/PMM/Security/SE prevented major claim drift.
- Evidence pack “index + request process” reduced ad-hoc doc hunting and lowered security review cycle time.

Bottlenecks
- Security questionnaire turnaround was inconsistent when asks landed via multiple channels (email + Slack + calls). We need a single intake and SLA.
- Packaging confusion on Dedicated vs Private (especially for VPC-private endpoints and data boundaries). We need a one-page internal decision tree and a consistent external explanation.

Product/docs feedback surfaced (non-scope, but high leverage)
- SAML metadata validation edge cases surfaced during pilot; document known constraints + recommended IdP configs.
- Audit log export formats and field-level definitions need a single canonical reference (docs + example snippet), especially for SIEM ingestion.
- Retention/residency language needs a standardized “what’s supported today vs by contract vs by configuration” section.

Proposed next steps (scale plan)
Recommendation: double down on FinServ first; keep Healthcare as a smaller, tightly-qualified motion.

Why FinServ first
- Faster path to “security call → POC” when framed as auditability + latency + predictable throughput.
- Higher incidence of Dedicated/Private fit signals (VPC, SIEM, KMS/HSM) without the additional PHI-specific program requirements.

90-day plan (v2 ABM)
1) Tighten the motion
- Create a single “Regulated Intake” channel + lightweight form for security questionnaires and evidence requests; commit to internal SLA (e.g., acknowledge in 1 business day, first pass in 3 business days).
- Publish an internal “Dedicated vs Private” decision tree + externally-safe explainer (no over-claims).

2) Refresh assets (minimum viable)
- Update landing page with 2 clearer architecture patterns (Dedicated in isolated environment vs Private VPC/on-prem).
- Add a security review prep checklist (what we’ll cover; what to bring) to reduce back-and-forth.
- Add a public-friendly proof point section (genericized benchmarks + operational outcomes). If no named customer logos, use anonymized “reference profile” format with Security-approved wording.

3) Expand targeting channels
- Add AWS Marketplace co-sell motions for accounts already in procurement processes; standardize private offer steps and who owns each step.
- Run 1 webinar or virtual roundtable only after the v2 landing page + proof point section is ready (avoid scaling top-of-funnel before mid-funnel is de-risked).

4) Measurement upgrades
- Enforce per-account “first conversion event” tracking (reply vs meeting vs evidence request vs security call) so we don’t over-credit meetings.
- Track cycle time metrics: first touch → first meeting, first meeting → security review, security review → POC start.

Decision points
- By end of next 2-week sprint: confirm v2 scope, owners, and publish the internal packaging decision tree.
- By day 45: decide whether Healthcare expands beyond “PHI-adjacent only” based on whether we can standardize retention/residency language and reduce legal review overhead.

Open questions / asks
- Security: confirm the minimum externally-shareable statements for retention/residency by deployment type; add to FAQ.
- Product (Private): confirm roadmap stance on audit export formats (what’s supported today vs planned).
- Sales leadership: confirm whether we want to gate “evidence pack” behind a meeting for ABM (improves qualification) or keep as request-based (improves conversion rate).

  1) Security FAQ (link sent in outbound follow-up) 
  2) Evidence pack index (created confidence + made the request process concrete)
  3) ABM deck (used in live calls; reduced time to “next step”)
- Landing page performance was “good enough” for ABM but not yet a scalable demand gen engine; the CTA that worked best was “Book a security + architecture review” over “Download benchmark.”

Messaging learnings (what worked / what we changed)
What worked
- Lead with auditability + control: audit logs, encryption/KMS, SSO/SAML, data handling boundaries.
- Pair compliance with economics: “predictable throughput and cost controls under real traffic,” not generic “fast GPUs.”
- Emphasize deployment flexibility as a risk reducer: Hosted → Dedicated → Private/VPC/on-prem as they mature.

What did not work (or triggered friction)
- “HIPAA-ready” language in healthcare; too loaded without strict qualification and legal review. Replaced with “PHI-adjacent workflows” + “supports common security controls; details depend on deployment and customer configuration.”
- Overly model-centric positioning; buyers cared more about operational posture (logs, access controls, change management, retention).

Top objections and effective responses
- “We need on-prem / no public internet access” → Redwood Private deployment pattern + customer-managed networking; SE-led architecture review.
- “We need audit logs exported to our SIEM” → show log fields + export options; clarify timeline for any format specifics; point to examples.
- “We need strict data retention controls” → clarify current retention policy per deployment and what is customer-configurable; align on requirements early.
- “We already have GPUs” → frame Redwood as the inference runtime + control plane + operational tooling (rollouts, fallback, observability) and faster path to SLA compliance.

Process learnings (internal)
What went well
- Weekly governance with Sales/PMM/Security/SE prevented major claim drift.
- Evidence pack “index + request process” reduced ad-hoc doc hunting and lowered security review cycle time.

Bottlenecks
- Security questionnaire turnaround was inconsistent when asks landed via multiple channels (email + Slack + calls). We need a single intake and SLA.
- Packaging confusion on Dedicated vs Private (especially for VPC-private endpoints and data boundaries). We need a one-page internal decision tree and a consistent external explanation.

Product/docs feedback surfaced (non-scope, but high leverage)
- SAML metadata validation edge cases surfaced during pilot; document known constraints + recommended IdP configs.
- Audit log export formats and field-level definitions need a single canonical reference (docs + example snippet), especially for SIEM ingestion.
- Retention/residency language needs a standardized “what’s supported today vs by contract vs by configuration” section.

Proposed next steps (scale plan)
Recommendation: double down on FinServ first; keep Healthcare as a smaller, tightly-qualified motion.

Why FinServ first
- Faster path to “security call → POC” when framed as auditability + latency + predictable throughput.
- Higher incidence of Dedicated/Private fit signals (VPC, SIEM, KMS/HSM) without the additional PHI-specific program requirements.

90-day plan (v2 ABM)
1) Tighten the motion
- Create a single “Regulated Intake” channel + lightweight form for security questionnaires and evidence requests; commit to internal SLA (e.g., acknowledge in 1 business day, first pass in 3 business days).
- Publish an internal “Dedicated vs Private” decision tree + externally-safe explainer (no over-claims).

2) Refresh assets (minimum viable)
- Update landing page with 2 clearer architecture patterns (Dedicated in isolated environment vs Private VPC/on-prem).
- Add a security review prep checklist (what we’ll cover; what to bring) to reduce back-and-forth.
- Add a public-friendly proof point section (genericized benchmarks + operational outcomes). If no named customer logos, use anonymized “reference profile” format with Security-approved wording.

3) Expand targeting channels
- Add AWS Marketplace co-sell motions for accounts already in procurement processes; standardize private offer steps and who owns each step.
- Run 1 webinar or virtual roundtable only after the v2 landing page + proof point section is ready (avoid scaling top-of-funnel before mid-funnel is de-risked).

4) Measurement upgrades
- Enforce per-account “first conversion event” tracking (reply vs meeting vs evidence request vs security call) so we don’t over-credit meetings.
- Track cycle time metrics: first touch → first meeting, first meeting → security review, security review → POC start.

Decision points
- By end of next 2-week sprint: confirm v2 scope, owners, and publish the internal packaging decision tree.
- By day 45: decide whether Healthcare expands beyond “PHI-adjacent only” based on whether we can standardize retention/residency language and reduce legal review overhead.

Open questions / asks
- Security: confirm the minimum externally-shareable statements for retention/residency by deployment type; add to FAQ.
- Product (Private): confirm roadmap stance on audit export formats (what’s supported today vs planned).
- Sales leadership: confirm whether we want to gate “evidence pack” behind a meeting for ABM (improves qualification) or keep as request-based (improves conversion rate).

title: Create regulated workloads landing page (ABM pilot)
preview:
Goal
Launch a customer-facing landing page tailored to security/compliance-driven buyers (finserv + healthcare/PHI-adjacent) as part of the 6–8 week Vertical ABM pilot. Page should route to the correct next step (security review call / architecture consult) and link to Sales-ready security materials without making over-broad compliance claims.

Primary CTA
“Request a security + architecture review” (form) with secondary CTA: “See security FAQ” (public doc) and gated CTA: “Request evidence pack” (sales-assisted / NDA).

Scope / Requirements
1) Page structure (target: single page, lightweight, skimmable)
- Hero: regulated positioning (performance + compliance posture) with 1 sentence on deployment options.
- Sections:
  - “Deploy the way regulated teams need”: Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private (VPC/on-prem) with clear guidance on when to choose each.
  - “Security & compliance controls”: encryption, key management, audit logs, SSO/SAML, retention/residency controls (wording must be Security-approved).
  - “Operational reliability”: SLAs (where applicable), predictable throughput, observability, rollouts/fallback.
  - “How a 2–3 week pilot works”: benchmark + architecture validation + security review timeline.
  - “FAQ”: short, link out to full security FAQ.
- Footnotes/clarifiers:
  - No “HIPAA-certified/HIPAA-compliant” or “guarantees” language.
  - Clarify shared responsibility (customer config + Redwood controls).

2) Copy guidelines (must align with PMM + Security)
- Must use the approved phrasing from PMM/Security review thread (see: sources/gmail/soojin_lee/20250504-messaging-review-regulated-workloads.json).
- Avoid absolute statements unless backed by evidence pack artifacts.
- Keep claims to capabilities (e.g., “supports audit logging” vs “meets all audit requirements”).

3) Design + UX
- Reuse existing docs site layout patterns where possible.
- Include “Talk to Security” routing for regulated buyers (minimize dead-ends).
- Ensure accessibility (contrast, headings, keyboard nav). (Design ticket: DES-882)

4) Measurement / Ops
- Add UTMs and conversion tracking (form submission + calendar booking).
- Capture persona + deployment interest on form:
  - Persona: Security/GRC, Platform/ML Eng, Procurement/IT
  - Deployment interest: Dedicated, Private (VPC), On-prem, Not sure
  - Region/residency requirement (optional)
- Owner for attribution spec: Tyler Benson.

5) Review checklist (hard gate before publish)
Security (Dr. Aisha Rahman / Naomi Feldman)
- Approved language for encryption, key management, audit logging, retention/residency, incident response.
- Confirm links to Security FAQ + evidence pack index are correct and access-controlled.
- Confirm no new commitments on timelines/SLAs.

Legal (Michael Grant / Sofia Mendes)
- Check disclaimers/footnotes and CTA wording.
- Ensure evidence pack request flow implies NDA where required.

Docs (Elliot Price / Kira Thompson)
- Style, IA, link hygiene, and maintenance owner for future edits.

Deliverables
- Published landing page on docs/marketing site (final URL TBD; align with existing docs routing).
- Cross-links:
  - Security FAQ: sources/confluence/sales-enablement/security-faq/regulated-workloads-security-faq.json
  - Evidence pack index (internal + process): sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/compliance-evidence/abm-pilot-security-evidence-pack-index.json
- Brief internal enablement blurb in Slack (#sales, #marketing) with approved snippet + link.

Implementation notes / Links
- Draft copy source: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/go-to-market/landing-page-copy-draft-regulated.json
- Docs PR (implementation): sources/github/redwood-docs/pr-421-add-regulated-workloads-vertical-landing-page.json
- Related positioning brief: sources/confluence/sales-enablement/positioning-and-messaging/vertical-abm-pilot-regulated-workloads-brief.json

Acceptance criteria
- Page is live and indexed where intended (or intentionally noindex if requested by Marketing during pilot).
- CTA form is functional and routes to correct owner queue.
- All security/compliance claims are explicitly approved by Security + Legal.
- Links to Security FAQ and evidence pack request instructions work.
- Tracking confirms at minimum: page views, CTA clicks, form submits.

Launch target
Publish by 2025-05-30 (end of prep window) to support Week 3 outreach; if delayed, publish no later than 2025-06-03 with comms to ABM owners.

preview: Design and run a pricing + contract experiment that clarifies when SSO/SAML and RBAC are treated as included features vs paid add-ons, and produce a contract language pack and security questionnaire guidance for sales/legal. Outcome: a repeatable packaging decision framework and a short-run pricing experiment to validate willingness-to-pay and sales friction.

title: Tiered Security Addendum & Pricing Experiment for SSO/SAML + RBAC
preview:
Problem statement: Enterprise customers frequently require contractual commitments for access control (SSO/SAML) and role-based access control (RBAC). Currently these appear as ad-hoc addenda negotiated during sales cycles which increases legal time and sales friction. We need a product-led packaging experiment that standardizes a small set of contractable security addenda tied to explicit pricing/commitment tiers so sales and legal can offer predictable options without lengthy negotiation.

Goals: 1) Define 2-3 standardized 'Security Addendum' tiers that can be attached to Hosted and Dedicated offerings: Basic (SSO/SAML support + standard RBAC), Enhanced (SLA-backed RBAC enforcement + audit log retention), and Isolated (private tenant + KMS integration + custom RBAC mappings). 2) Determine attachment pricing or commitment thresholds for each tier and run an experiment with a subset of pipeline customers to validate willingness-to-pay and contract velocity impact. 3) Produce proposed contract clause language, checklist for Security Questionnaire responses, and an approved sales playbook for offering the tiers.

Scope: Includes hosted API and Redwood Dedicated. Excludes full on-prem appliance packaging and custom engineering for bespoke RBAC models; those remain enterprise professional services work.

Assumptions: - Most customers prefer a small menu of options vs bespoke legal addenda. - Pricing signal should be conservative for initial experiment to reduce friction. - Security questionnaire answers must map directly to the tier capabilities to reduce legal back-and-forth.

Planned experiment: Draft tier definitions and pricing -> legal review for clause language -> pilot offer to 8 inbound enterprise trials (sales will include a simple A/B: standard negotiation vs pick-a-tier) -> measure contract cycle time, acceptance rate, average ARR uplift, and number of redlines.

title: Consolidate SIG/CAIQ responses and standardized customer attestations for pricing tiers
preview: Goal: Create a single authoritative SIG/CAIQ response bundle and a set of tiered customer attestation templates that map to Redwood pricing/packaging tiers. This reduces sales cycles, ensures consistent legal posture across deals, and enables automated artifact generation for self-serve and sales-assisted flows. Scope: inventory existing responses, reconcile conflicts between engineering artifacts and legal guidance, produce a canonical CAIQ answer pack (v1) plus three attestation templates (Self-Serve, Dedicated, Private) with agreed scoping language. Deliverables: canonical CAIQ JSON+PDF, three attestation templates, a lightweight sales playbook page, a generator script to inject customer-specific values, and a legal sign-off checklist.

title: Enterprise dedicated request gating and prioritization framework
preview:
Problem: We receive ad-hoc enterprise requests for Dedicated (reserved) capacity that vary widely in SLA expectations, throughput requirements, and hardware compatibility. Currently intake lacks consistent gating criteria, leading to mismatched capacity commits, last-minute engineering scope increases, and elevated operational risk.

Goal: Define a lightweight but rigorous intake and prioritization framework that product and solutions teams can use to triage enterprise Dedicated requests. title: Enterprise staging pin reservation & risk-buffer playbook for model catalog onboarding
preview:
Problem: Enterprise customers frequently request model pins and new model additions that have high business impact but varying risk profiles (latency, PII exfiltration surface, quantization quality). Current intake is binary (approve/reject) which causes either expensive emergency rollbacks or lengthy hold times. Proposal: introduce an 'Enterprise Staging Pin Reservation' playbook that creates a temporary, time-boxed pin with an attached risk-buffer and mandatory eval gates. This playbook standardizes decision criteria, operational controls, and provides a fast but safe path to grant enterprises short-term stability while the model completes full onboarding.

Key goals:
- Reduce mean time to pin approval for high-value enterprise requests from days to <48 hours for staged pins.
- Prevent long-lived, unreviewed pins by enforcing automatic expiry and deprecation triggers.
- Tie pin issuance to quantifiable eval gates (quality regression, latency, estimated cost delta, security scan).
- Provide clear rollback and fallback policies to protect customer SLAs and platform SLOs.

Proposal summary:
1) Staged Pin Reservation: when a customer with Enterprise tier requests a pin, PM can approve a staged pin valid for N days (default 14). This reservation is visible in the catalog and in the Console.
2) Risk Buffer: each staged pin includes a risk rating (Low/Medium/High) and an operational buffer—e.g., traffic throttles, dedicated fallback model, and a KV-cache warmup period.
3) Eval Gate Bundle: a mandatory checklist (automated and manual) that must be completed during the staged period to convert the staged pin into a permanent pin. Failure to pass gates triggers automatic rollback and a post-mortem.
4) Pin Expiry and Escalation: unconverted staged pins auto-expire; PM triggers escalation and customer communication.
5) Pilot: roll out to 3 enterprise customers with diverse workload profiles (chat/embeddings/rerank) before wider launch.

Why different from existing intake flows: this creates a middle lane between fast-hosted pinning and full catalog onboarding, balancing time-to-stability with controls required by compliance and SRE.

title: Geofence AuditStream: automated control-to-artifact mapping for data residency & retention evidence
preview:
Goal: build an automated 'AuditStream' that maps residency and retention controls to tamper-evident artifacts so SOC 2 / ISO 27001 auditors and customers can rapidly verify compliance for region-bound datasets.

Scope: produce evidence for three common control families: (1) Geofencing (data never leaves region X), (2) Retention policy enforcement (data deleted after N days, retention exceptions recorded), (3) Evidence collection & signing (artifact generation, hash anchoring, and access claims). This ticket covers the PM-level orchestration, control mapping matrix, acceptance criteria, and rollout plan. Implementation split into collectors (cloud telemetry, object metadata sweeps), an aggregator service that assembles artifacts, and a signer/manifest service that timestamps and stores proofs in a tamper-evident store.

Design notes: artifacts will be compact JSON manifests containing: data scope descriptor, selector queries, evidence snapshot (counts, sample keys), retention state, KMS-wrapped signature, and a digest anchored to an append-only store (e.g., ledger S3 with object lock + digest index). Collector triggers: scheduled (daily) + on-demand (customer request / legal hold). Evidence TTL: artifacts retained for 7 years by default for audit trails but surfaced to customers with configurable retention windows in the console.

Non-goals: this ticket does not include UI polish for the public customer-facing evidence packages nor Dedicated on-prem packaging; those will be follow-ups.

title: Migration Accelerator: Beta Cohorts, In-Product Nudge Flows, and Release Notes Kit
preview:
Goal: Deliver a reusable Migration Accelerator playbook and asset kit to make deprecations predictable, low-friction, and measurable for customers. This ticket covers three deliverables: 1) Beta cohort selection and operations checklist (segmentation, invite cadence, success criteria); 2) In-product nudge flows and SDK warnings (banner copy, throttled reminders, SDK deprecation warnings, telemetry hooks); 3) Release notes kit and GTM cadence (email templates, blog + changelog entries, support-runbook, PRD for console surfaced warnings).

Scope: We are planning a staged sunset of api/v1-models to api/v2-models for a subset of legacy model variants. This is not a full platform-wide retirement. Workstreams include: cohort design (which customers to enroll for early migration assistance), in-product UX (console banners, modal flows), SDK/CLI update strategy (deprecation headers and opt-in shims), comms (multi-channel templates), metrics (migration funnel tracking, error/regression monitors), and rollback/fallback policies (automatic model-variant fallback + SLO gates).

Success metrics: 60% of targeted cohort migrated within 6 weeks of invite; <1% spike in error-rate attributable to migration; customer satisfaction NPS change <= -3; migration automation reduces manual support touches by 50% for enrolled customers.

Non-goals: This ticket does not include private deployment upgrade tooling (that's covered under PM-PRIVATE-UPGRADES) nor changes to runtime kernel selection. It also does not change pricing or hard cutover dates for non-enrolled customers.

Key stakeholders: Product (owner), Solutions Eng (migration concierge), DevRel (migration guides), Docs (release notes templates), Support (runbook + escalation path), Eng Reliability (telemetry + rollback), Legal/Trust (if required for customer comms).

title: Competitive compass: translating model-landscape signals into procurement & fallback decisions
preview: Problem: As the open-model ecosystem proliferates, our commercial teams and platform ops need a repeatable way to map external signals about model availability, pricing, latency, and feature drift into concrete procurement actions (reserve vs hosted), pricing guidance, and automated fallback rules. Scope: produce a signal-to-decision matrix, an operational rubric that weights signals (e.g., availability volatility, quantization quality, regional latency delta, contractual SLA gaps), and a one-page procurement decision playbook that can be used by PMs, sales-engineering, and Platform SREs. Deliverables: 1) prioritized signal taxonomy with weights and examples, 2) decision matrix mapping signal bands to procurement actions and fallback policies, 3) playbook doc + template RFP checklist for enterprise deals, 4) instrumentation spec for ops (what to surface in Console and Alerts). This ticket is to run the cross-functional discovery, synthesize the first iteration, and ship the playbook artifact for review.

title: Pinning playbook: operator UX and policies for model-version pin + graceful fallback
preview:
Problem: As teams adopt model version pinning and staged rollouts (canary / A-B), interactions across region selection, capacity tiers (hosted/dedicated/private), and model variant fallbacks create subtle production behaviors that increase error rates, latency variability, and surprise cost exposure. This ticket proposes a product playbook and operator UX requirements to make pinning + rollout behavior explicit, safe, and observable for customers.

Goals:
- Define deterministic policy semantics that compose: region preference, tier ordering (dedicated -> hosted -> private fallback), version pin TTL and cohort rules, and model-variant degrade paths.
- Surface these policies in Console and API with clear defaults and override knobs for advanced users.
- Provide monitoring, alerts, and bounded cost/latency SLAs during rollouts.
- Ship a customer-facing runbook "Pin + Rollout Safety Playbook" and an initial Console UI to visualize active pin cohorts and fallback chains.

Scope (deliverables):
1) Playbook doc that describes canonical behaviors and operator decisions.
2) API params & Console UI spec for: pin TTL, cohort seed percentage, deterministic vs probabilistic affinity, sticky session window, fallback priority list (region, tier, variant).
3) Default policy recommendations and safe guardrails (e.g., max canary qps, cost cap, automatic rollback triggers).
4) E2E acceptance tests and dashboards: per-cohort latency/cost/regression meters.
5) Migration notes for customers using legacy pin semantics.

Non-goals: Full implementation of server-side deterministic shard pinning (engineering will implement separately). Initial Console work should be read-only visualization + controls for enabling playbook policies and toggles; advanced runtime behavior implemented via integration with orchestrator RFCs.

title: Reservation authorization and capacity workbench for Dedicated pools
preview:
Goal: Define and implement a bounded, auditable reservation request and approval workflow plus an operator workbench that ties throughput guarantees, autoscaling policy templates, and fleet controls into the Dedicated provisioning lifecycle. This ticket scopes the PM deliverables: request form schema, approval matrix, capacity planning heuristics, UX sketches for the workbench, and the verification playbook for provisioning events.

Context: Several early customers are adopting Dedicated and ask for a single place to request guaranteed throughput (tokens/sec or requests/sec), choose autoscaling behaviors (aggressive, balanced, conservative), and indicate fleet constraints (allowed regions, GPU families, maintenance windows). Engineers want predictable capacity sizing and test hooks to validate SLOs after an allocation. Current ad-hoc requests cause back-and-forth between sales, SRE, and infra and increase lead time.

Scope (what we will deliver in this ticket):
- Definitive reservation request schema (fields, validations, default values).
- Approval matrix that maps request attributes (size, contract value, security needs) to required approvers and SLAs.
- Autoscaling policy templates (3 presets + custom) with recommended defaults for GPU pool sizes, target utilization, cooldowns, burst capacity and drain behavior.
- Fleet control knobs and constraints (tagging, allowed racks/regions, maintenance windows, preemption policy).
- Capacity planning heuristics and forecasting doc to convert expected QPS/seq_len/traffic profile into GPU unit reservation and cost estimate.
- Operator workbench wireframes and flows (review queue, approve/deny with comments, rollback, test harness link).
- Verification & onboarding checklist (smoke tests, perf benchmarks, KV cache warmup, canary traffic ramp).

Out of scope: billing contract language, long-term SLA legal framework, deep infra implementation (this ticket produces the product spec and acceptance tests; ENG tickets will follow).

title: Console policy composer — region priorities, tier routing, and staged model fallbacks
preview: Build a customer-facing Policy Composer in the Redwood Console that lets customers express routing preferences across regions, capacity tiers (hot/warm/cold), and ordered fallback model variants. The composer should be approachable for PM/Dev users (visual rule blocks + guided defaults) while remaining expressive for advanced users (JSON/DLS export/import). title: Tranche Playbook for Enterprise-Isolated Deploys: Quarterly Cadence, Risk Budget, and Acceptance Gates
preview:
Summary: Create a repeatable, tranche-based quarterly playbook for delivering Private / VPC / on-prem features to enterprise customers. The playbook will define tranche boundaries (canary -> wave -> general availability), a risk budget model (time, rollback windows, compatibility debt), acceptance gates, cross-functional roles/responsibilities, and observability/signals tied to rollout decisions.

Scope:
- Operational playbook for sequencing quarterly deployments across gated tranches (Tier 1 canaries, Tier 2 waves, Tier 3 GA).
- Standardized acceptance criteria and telemetry thresholds for promoting between tranches.
- Rollback and fallback policies mapped to model/runtime versions, upgrade windows, and customer cohorts.
- Communication templates, runbook checklist, and stakeholder cadence (PM/Eng/Support/Customer Success).
- Risk budget spreadsheet and escalation paths for SLA degradations, security findings, and compatibility regressions.

Out of scope: day-to-day incident response procedures (will link to infra runbooks), individual customer onboarding steps (handled by CS playbooks).

title: Interim usage quarantine and automated tenant-credit simulator for contested enterprise invoices
preview: Summary: Enterprise customers sometimes raise security questionnaire or contract questions that result in a temporary hold/escrow on invoices while usage is investigated. Current operations require manual ledger edits and provisional credits created by Finance, which is slow and error-prone for high-volume metered accounts. This ticket scopes a short-lived product/ops capability: (1) a usage quarantine flow that isolates suspect usage from final invoicing, and (2) an automated tenant-credit simulator (sandbox) that lets CS/Finance/PMs preview prorations, provisional credits, and final invoice outcomes before accepting/committing ledger changes. Goal: reduce mean time-to-resolution for contested usage, lower manual adjustment errors, and provide reproducible experiments for pricing/packaging A/Bs that touch credits/proration logic.
